questions.question: Explain the need for page tables in a paging system. What information is typically stored in a page table entry (PTE)?
questions.hint: Consider what information the OS needs to translate a virtual address to physical address
keyPoints: Page tables map virtual page numbers to physical frame numbers
PTE contains valid/invalid bit
PTE contains frame number (physical page number)
PTE contains protection bits (read/write/execute)
PTE may contain reference bit and modified (dirty) bit
PTE may contain caching disable bits

questions.question: What is a hierarchical page table? Explain with a diagram how two-level paging works.
questions.hint: Think of splitting the virtual page number into two parts for two-level paging
keyPoints: Hierarchical page table divides the virtual address into multiple parts
Outer page table points to inner page tables
Inner page tables point to physical frames
Two-level paging: first level index into outer page table, second level index into inner page table
Reduces memory overhead for sparse address spaces
Example: 32-bit address with 10-bit outer and 10-bit inner index

questions.question: Compare and contrast hierarchical page table with inverted page table.
questions.hint: Focus on memory usage and access speed differences
keyPoints: Hierarchical: one entry per virtual page, indexed by virtual page number
Inverted: one entry per physical frame, indexed by physical frame number
Inverted page table reduces memory overhead significantly
Inverted table requires hashing for efficient search
Hierarchical provides faster direct access but uses more memory
Inverted table has slower lookup due to hash collision handling

questions.question: What is a Translation Lookaside Buffer (TLB)? Explain its role in address translation.
questions.hint: TLB is a special cache for page table entries
keyPoints: TLB is a hardware cache for page table entries
Stores recently used virtual to physical address translations
Contains key (VPN) and value (PFN) pairs
Reduces memory accesses during address translation
On TLB hit: single memory access to get data
On TLB miss: page table walk required, then TLB updated

questions.question: Explain the structure of a hashed page table. For what type of address spaces is it particularly useful?
questions.hint: Consider virtual address spaces that are very large but sparsely used
keyPoints: Hashed page table uses hash function on virtual page number
Hash table maps VPN to linked list of entries
Each entry in list contains VPN, PFN, and pointer to next entry
Useful for sparse address spaces (large virtual address space)
Reduces memory overhead compared to flat page table
Handles hash collisions through chaining

questions.question: What are the protection bits associated with a page table entry? Explain their significance.
questions.hint: Consider both access control and usage tracking
keyPoints: Valid/Invalid bit indicates whether the page is in physical memory
Read, Write, Execute bits control access permissions
User/Supervisor bit distinguishes kernel from user pages
Modified (Dirty) bit shows if page was modified
Referenced (Access) bit indicates if page was recently accessed
Protection bits help implement memory protection and prevent unauthorized access

questions.question: Design a two-level page table structure for a 32-bit virtual address space with 4KB page size. Show how the virtual address is divided and explain the page table entry format.
keyPoints: 4KB page size means 12 bits for offset (2^12 = 4096)
32-12 = 20 bits for virtual page number
Split 20 bits into two levels: 10 bits for outer, 10 bits for inner
Outer page table has 2^10 = 1024 entries
Inner page table has 2^10 = 1024 entries
Each PTE size: assume 4 bytes (32 bits) for frame number + status bits
Outer PTE: points to inner page table base address
Inner PTE: contains physical frame number
Address translation: use first 10 bits to index outer PT, next 10 bits to index inner PT, last 12 bits as offset

questions.question: Explain the different techniques for handling TLB misses. Discuss the roles of hardware and software in TLB miss handling.
questions.hint: Consider who performs the page table walk - hardware or OS
keyPoints: Hardware TLB miss handling: MMU walks the page table
Software TLB miss handling: OS page table walker (trap handler)
Hardware approach: faster but complex MMU design
Software approach: more flexible, easier to modify page table format
TLB reload: update TLB with correct entry after miss
TLB coherence issues in multiprocessor systems
TLB flush operations during context switch

questions.question: What is an inverted page table? Explain its working with a suitable diagram. What are its advantages and disadvantages compared to a traditional page table?
questions.hint: Think about indexing by physical frame number instead of virtual page number
keyPoints: Inverted page table has one entry per physical frame
Entry contains: frame number, owning process ID, virtual page number
Search performed using process ID and virtual page number
Uses hash table for efficient lookup
Advantages: scales well for large address spaces, less memory usage
Disadvantages: slower lookup due to hashing, more complex, cannot easily share pages
Memory overhead: O(physical frames) instead of O(virtual pages)

questions.question: Discuss the concept of memory protection using page tables. How do page table entries help in implementing protection?
questions.hint: Consider how different bits in PTE work together for protection
keyPoints: Page tables provide isolation between processes
Each process has its own page table (separate address spaces)
Protection bits in PTE control access levels
Valid bit prevents access to pages not in memory
Read/Write bits prevent unauthorized modifications
User/Supervisor bit separates kernel and user mode access
Page fault occurs on protection violation
Enables copy-on-write and shared read-only pages

questions.question: Explain the concept of virtual memory. How does the page table structure support virtual memory implementation?
questions.hint: Key is the valid/invalid bit and its role in page faults
keyPoints: Virtual memory creates illusion of larger physical memory
Pages can be placed in physical memory or on disk (swap)
Valid bit indicates if page is in memory or not
Reference bit helps in page replacement algorithms
Modified bit indicates if page needs to be written to disk before eviction
Page fault occurs on invalid page access, OS loads page from disk
Page tables enable demand paging and swapping
Structure supports efficient page replacement

questions.question: A computer system has a 32-bit virtual address space and uses a two-level page table. The page size is 4KB and each page table entry is 4 bytes. Calculate: (a) Number of bits for offset, (b) Size of each page table, (c) Total size of page table hierarchy if all second-level tables are present.
questions.hint: First find how the virtual address is divided
keyPoints: Page size 4KB = 2^12, so offset bits = 12
Virtual page number bits = 32 - 12 = 20 bits
Split into two 10-bit parts for two-level paging
Each second-level page table has 2^10 = 1024 entries
Each PTE = 4 bytes, so each second-level PT size = 1024 × 4 = 4KB
Number of second-level tables = 2^10 = 1024
Total second-level size = 1024 × 4KB = 4MB
Outer page table: 2^10 entries × 4 bytes = 4KB
Total hierarchy size = 4KB + 4MB ≈ 4MB

questions.question: Consider a system with TLB that has a hit rate of 80%. The memory access time is 100ns and TLB lookup takes 20ns. Calculate the effective memory access time (a) without TLB, (b) with TLB. Also calculate the speedup factor.
questions.hint: Consider both hit and miss cases weighted by their probabilities
keyPoints: Without TLB: need 2 memory accesses (one for page table, one for data) = 2 × 100 = 200ns
With TLB hit: TLB lookup + 1 memory access = 20 + 100 = 120ns
With TLB miss: TLB lookup + 2 memory accesses = 20 + 200 = 220ns
Alternative formula: T_eff = hit_rate × (TLB + mem) + miss_rate × (TLB + 2×mem)

questions.question: A system uses inverted page table with hashing. The hash table has 256 entries and uses chaining for collision resolution. If the system has 128 physical frames and the hash function is h(vpn) = vpn mod 256, calculate: (a) Average chain length if all virtual pages are uniformly distributed, (b) What happens when multiple virtual pages hash to the same entry?
questions.hint: Hash function distributes virtual pages across hash table entries
keyPoints: Hash table entries = 256
Physical frames = 128
Average chain length = 128/256 = 0.5 entries per bucket initially
But typically more virtual pages than physical frames in system
If N virtual pages map to 256 buckets: average chain = N/256
Multiple VPN mapping to same index causes chaining
Each entry in chain contains: VPN, PID, frame number, next pointer
Longer chains mean slower lookup (linear search through chain)

questions.question: What are the issues with very large page tables? Discuss at least three techniques to handle this problem.
questions.hint: Think about memory efficiency vs access speed tradeoffs
keyPoints: Problem: Large address spaces lead to enormous page tables
Technique 1: Hierarchical/Multi-level paging - splits into smaller tables
Technique 2: Inverted page table - one entry per physical frame
Technique 3: Hashing - hash table instead of array
Technique 4: Page table compression - store only valid entries
Problem: All these add complexity and access time overhead
